narrative:
I'm sorry to deliver this news, but your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 77% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, based on patterns from thousands of past students. You have a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.3, which is above the typical student's 3.21, and an impressive LSAT score of 41, well above the average of 36.7—these are strengths that stand out compared to most applicants. However, your school decile rankings paint a different picture: in Year 1, you ranked in the 5th decile (slightly below the average of 5.71), and in Year 3, you were in the 3rd decile (considerably lower than the typical 5.53), suggesting inconsistent academic performance relative to your peers. Your family comes from a middle-income bracket, similar to many students, and as a white male planning full-time studies, you fit the most common demographic profile in the dataset.

When the model weighed all this, your Year 3 decile ranking had the strongest influence, pushing the prediction firmly toward a higher risk of bar failure. Right behind it was your Year 1 decile, also nudging the odds toward failure in a very similar way. Your LSAT score came next in influence, surprisingly contributing toward the failure prediction despite its strength, followed by family income, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status—all of which played smaller roles but still tilted things negatively. None of your traits pulled strongly in the direction of passing, which is why the overall risk came out so high compared to students whose profiles balance out more evenly.

In essence, while your test scores shine brighter than average, your lower school rankings in later years were the dominant signals of concern, outweighing those positives for this model. To boost your future bar exam chances, focus on building stronger, consistent academic habits now—perhaps through targeted tutoring or study strategies that could have lifted those deciles, as school performance was the top predictor here. Retaking the LSAT isn't likely needed given your score, but dedicating time to bar-specific prep courses could address the gaps the model flagged in your profile. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by prioritizing rigorous practice exams and time management, turning early warning signs into bar-passing realities. You're already ahead in key areas, so with focused effort on consistency, you can shift your odds dramatically. Keep pushing—you've got the foundation to make this right.
